entries.title: Illinois conversion therapy ban intact after Supreme Court ruling, though advocates wary of future challenges
entries.summary: <p>As a gay teen growing up in Benton, Illinois, Curtis Lopez-Galloway would be driven hours across state lines into Kentucky to see a religious counselor who worked out of his home.</p><p>The man would give him “masculine” activities to complete and derided a “homosexual lifestyle" that he claimed showed a predisposition to traits shared by murderers and pedophiles. According to Lopez-Galloway, the counselor eventually forced him to separate from friends who affirmed his sexuality, and instigated fights between him and his parents.</p><p>Lopez-Galloway said he eventually patched things up with his parents, but he still suffers from anxiety and depression.</p><p>“It was the darkest time of my life,” Lopez-Galloway said. “There’s always going to be a permanent scar on my relationship with my parents … it’s always there in the background.”</p><p>Now, at 31-years-old and having since founded the Conversion Therapy Survivor Network, Lopez-Galloway is still processing last week's Supreme Court <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/supreme-court-conversion-therapy-colorado-92b34295f9ef497a4a1cbeb56c9b74c6" target="_blank" ><u>ruling against a law banning “conversion therapy”</u></a> for LGBTQ+ kids in Colorado. It's one of 23 states — including Illinois — that ban the discredited practice, in addition to four that restrict it.</p><p>“I'm surprised it didn't happen earlier,” Lopez-Galloway said, while reiterating that Illinois' ban remains untouched "for now."</p><p>An 8-1 high court majority sided with a Christian counselor who argued the law banning talk therapy violates the First Amendment. The justices agreed that the law raises free speech concerns and sent it back to a lower court to decide if it meets a legal standard that few laws pass, though it left other bans intact.</p><p>It's the latest in a line of recent cases in which the justices have backed <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/supreme-court-mail-carrier-religious-discrimination-4d0b161349846f8e3bbcd6376059065f" target="_blank" >claims of religious discrimination</a> while taking a skeptical view of LGBTQ+ rights.</p><p>In her solo dissent, Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson wrote that states should be free to regulate health care, even if that means incidental restrictions on speech. The decision “opens a dangerous can of worms” that “threatens to impair states’ ability to regulate the provision of medical care in any respect,” she wrote.</p><p>The decision was immediately decried by health care provider organizations such the National Association of Social Workers, as well as the local Illinois chapter, who condemned the decision and said the practice “causes documented psychological damage.”</p><p>“No ethical social worker or mental health clinician should be engaging in this practice, full stop, regardless of what any court decides,” said Kyle Hillman, Director of Legislative Affairs for the group’s Illinois chapter, in a statement.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lgbtq/2025/06/26/10-years-after-winning-marriage-equality-lgbtq-chicagoans-look-back-on-their-fight-and-ahead" >10 years after winning marriage equality, LGBTQ+ Chicagoans look back on their fight — and ahead</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lgbtq/2025/07/23/sleepaway-camp-queer-kids-safety-community-lgbtq" >How a sleepaway camp became a ‘dream utopia’ for queer kids under threat</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lgbtq/2026/03/29/northwestern-university-study-trans-gender-affirming-care-dysphoria-michael-bailey" >A Northwestern study claims to look into trans youths’ well-being; parents and advocates warn it’ll bring harm</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Even still, the decision has raised questions about what challenges could crop up in the future.</p><p>Illinois’ law previously withstood a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2016/8/13/18352692/pastors-sue-illinois-over-gay-conversion-therapy-ban" target="_blank" ><u>similar challenge</u></a> on First Amendment grounds, though since it came specifically from clergy who were concerned their speech would be stunted by the law, the court tossed the federal suit. The bill’s authors specifically stated it didn’t regulate religious counseling, only applying to licensed providers in the state.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div
